solution.content:
[TOC]

## Solution
---
#### Overview ####

The problem is quite simple.For a given $$n$$, we have to build an array of size $$n+1$$ using a particular sequence generation pattern which is called [Stern's Diatomic Sequence](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Calkin%E2%80%93Wilf_tree#Stern's_diatomic_sequence). According to the given array generation rules, we could use the values at position $$i$$ and $$i+1$$ in array, to calculate the values for positions $$(i*2)$$ and $$(i*2+1)$$.
The following figure illustrates the idea.

![img](../Figures/5561/array_generation_overview.png)


Thus, we could build the array by sequentially traversing the array from index $$1$$ to index $$n$$ and use the values at the current index to calculate the next values. Once the array is generated, we have to return the maximum value.

Let's look at the approaches in detail.

---

#### Approach 1: Naive Solution

**Intuition**

The straightforward solution is to generate the array of size $$n+1$$ using the given generation pattern and keep track of the maximum element in the array.
For every array element in array `nums` at index `i` with value `nums[i]`, we must calculate the value at next positions as follows,
- `nums[i*2]` would be equal to value of `nums[i]`.
- `nums[i*2+1]` would be equal to the value of `nums[i] + nums[i+1]`.

The figure illustrates the array generated for a value of $$n$$ as $$8$$.

![img](../Figures/5561/array_generation_rules.png)


Using the value at index $$2$$, we could calculate value at index $$4$$ and using value at index $$2$$ and $$3$$, we could calculate the value at index $$5$$.


**Algorithm**
- Build an array `nums` of size `n+1`.
- Initialize `nums[1]` as `1` as specified in the problem statement.
- Iterate from `1` to `n` where `i` is the current index of array `nums`. As we iterate over each array element, calculate the value of positions `i*2` and `i*2+1`.
- Update the maximum value generated so far by comparing it with the value at the current index.

**Implementation**
<iframe src="https://leetcode.com/playground/ZPHUjz2p/shared" frameBorder="0" width="100%" height="446" name="ZPHUjz2p"></iframe>

**Complexity Analysis**

- Time Complexity : $$\mathcal{O}(n)$$, as we iterate $$n$$ times to build the array.

- Space Complexity: $$\mathcal{O}(n)$$, as we use an array $$\text{nums}$$ of size $$n$$.
---

#### Approach 2: Optimized Solution

**Intuition**

We aim to find the maximum value in the array. We make the following observation of the array generation algorithm,
- Using every array index `i`, we are finding the values for `i*2` and `i*2+1` positions. Hence, we could stop our traversal when the current index is equal to `n/2`, as `n/2 * 2` would be the last index (`n`) of the array and there is no point in going further as we have calculated all the results at this point.

![img](../Figures/5561/optimized_solution.png)


- If we are at index `i` and the maximum value calculated so far is `maximumValue`, the next maximum value could only be at the position `i*2+1`. Since the value at index `i*2` is always equal to the value at index `i`, we know that it would never be greater than the already calculated `maximumValue` value.

**Algorithm**
- Build an array `nums` of size `n+1` using the array generation rules as in _Approach 1_.
- The maximum value would only be updated if the value at position `nums[i*2+1]` is greater than the current maximum value. As the value gets incremented at that position, there is a chance of finding a new maximum value.
- We must iterate from index `1` to `n/2`. Since at `n/2` index, we have already calculated the values of all the elements till `n` index.


**Implementation**

<iframe src="https://leetcode.com/playground/QthbPuqa/shared" frameBorder="0" width="100%" height="395" name="QthbPuqa"></iframe>

**Complexity Analysis**

- Time Complexity : $$\mathcal{O}(n)$$, as we iterate $$n/2$$ times, the time complexity would be $$\mathcal{O}(n)$$.

- Space Complexity: $$\mathcal{O}(n)$$, as we use an array $$\text{nums}$$ of size $$n$$.

---

#### Approach 3: Using Division

**Intuition**

There is another way of solving this problem. Instead of generating the next values based on the current value (using multiplication), we could generate current value based on previous values (using division). Let's look at the array generation rules,

- `nums[i*2]` is equal to the value of `nums[i]`.

   This could be written as, if `i` is even,`nums[i]` is equal to the value of `nums[i/2]`

- `nums[i*2+1]` is equal to the value of `nums[i] + nums[i+1]`.

   This could be written as, if `i` is odd,`nums[i]` is equal to value of `nums[i/2] + nums[i/2+1]`

**Algorithm**
- Build an array `nums` of size `n+1` using the array generation rules.
- Iterate from `1` to `n` where `i` is the current index of array `nums`. As we iterate over each array element, calculate the value of current position `nums[i]` as follows,

   -  If current index `i` is even, the value of `nums[i]` is equal to `nums[i/2]`.
   -  Else, the value of `nums[i]` is equal to `nums[i/2] + nums[i/2+1]`.

- The maximum value would only be updated if the value at the current position `nums[i]` is greater than the maximum value calculated so far.

**Implementation**

<iframe src="https://leetcode.com/playground/5MpoUnop/shared" frameBorder="0" width="100%" height="395" name="5MpoUnop"></iframe>

**Complexity Analysis**

- Time Complexity : $$\mathcal{O}(n)$$, as we iterate $$n$$ times to build the array.

- Space Complexity: $$\mathcal{O}(n)$$, as we use an array $$\text{nums}$$ of size $$n$$.
